summary: Thoughtworks conducted 41 Ruby projects between 2006-2008, finding it a highly viable platform for web development. Technical leads overwhelmingly approved of choosing Ruby (36 to 5), citing productivity gains of 25-100% compared to Java/.NET, though they cautioned that social resistance from clients was the biggest red flag. While Ruby's execution speed is slow, this proved irrelevant for most I/O-bound web applications. The company learned important lessons about testing strategies, deployment approaches, and managing the meta-programming learning curve.
key_points: Ruby delivered measurable productivity gains, with most projects reporting 25-100% improvement over mainstream platforms, though teams need time to overcome the 'Improvement Ravine' when starting
The biggest barrier to Ruby adoption was social resistance from client organizations, not technical limitations
Ruby's slow execution speed was irrelevant for nearly all projects since web applications are typically I/O-bound by database access
Teams experience a predictable progression with meta-programming: from 'scary and bad' to overuse, eventually settling on judicious application
Ruby works well as a supplementary scripting language for Java/.NET projects, supporting polyglot programming approaches
money_quotes: Ruby has a philosophy of an environment that gives a talented developer more leverage, rather than trying to protect a less talented developer from errors.

The difference is between efficiency risk and delivery risk. A team with dynamic language experience but little Ruby experience will be slower initially (efficiency risk) but a team without any dynamic language experience can produce a knotty code base that could risk the overall delivery.
Choosing a development platform is never a simple choice, particularly since it usually is more of a social choice than a technological choice.
The analogy I like best for these kinds of techniques is that they are like prescription drugs. They are very valuable in small amounts but you need to ensure that you don't overdose.
When learning something new it's common to over-use it at some stage because without crossing the line it's hard to know where that line is.
The point of these frameworks is to improve productivity by making the easier to do common things. It allows a team to focus its effort on the few cases that really matter.
key_insight: Technology platform choices are fundamentally social decisions, and Ruby's value proposition lies in amplifying skilled developers rather than constraining less capable ones.
